title: Aion Integration Surface
intention:
When this cluster is complete, Aion dispatches activities, delivers signals, and streams workflow history entirely through liminal conversations and channels. The gRPC worker dispatch layer is gone. A workflow author writes schedule_activity and the bus handles routing, failure detection, and re-dispatch. An operator scales the worker pool by starting or stopping worker processes -- no rebalancing, no configuration changes, no downtime. The zero-hop embedded path means a beamr instance running both Aion and liminal dispatches activities without crossing a network boundary, and the same code path works identically when workers run on remote nodes connected via beamr distribution.

This is the cluster that proves the layer cake: haematite stores durable state, liminal mediates communication, Aion orchestrates workflows. If this integration surface is clean, every future consumer of liminal (the messaging product, the real-time channels product) inherits the same patterns.
problem:
Aion currently dispatches activities to workers via gRPC. This creates three problems.

First, worker failure detection is slow. gRPC relies on connection-level health checks and deadline timeouts. A worker that crashes mid-activity is not detected until the deadline expires (seconds to minutes), during which the workflow is stalled. In contrast, beamr process links detect termination in microseconds.

Second, scaling the worker pool requires configuration changes. Adding a worker means updating the gRPC endpoint list and potentially triggering consumer group rebalancing. Removing a worker risks in-flight activity loss if the drain protocol is not followed exactly. The operational surface is large and error-prone.

Third, the gRPC transport is a separate infrastructure dependency. Aion workflows running in the same beamr instance as the workers still cross a network boundary (localhost loopback) for every activity dispatch. This adds latency, serialization overhead, and a failure mode (port exhaustion, connection limits) that should not exist for co-located components.

Signal delivery and workflow history streaming have analogous problems: signals go through a separate RPC path, and history consumers poll the event store directly, coupling them to the storage layer's API and creating read contention under load.
solution:
The integration surface is a Rust module at crates/liminal/src/aion/ that provides the bridge between Aion's workflow engine and liminal's channel and conversation primitives. It does not modify Aion internals or liminal core -- it is a consumer of both APIs.

Activity Dispatch

Activity dispatch replaces gRPC with conversation-mediated routing per ADR-007. When a workflow calls schedule_activity, the integration layer opens a dispatch conversation on the activity's task queue channel. The dispatch conversation is a supervised beamr process that selects a worker from the pool via the channel's routing function, links to the selected worker process, sends the activity request, and waits for completion. Worker crash is detected immediately via the process link -- the conversation catches the exit signal and re-dispatches to another worker without waiting for a timeout. The routing function has access to worker capacity declarations, enabling load-aware dispatch without a central coordinator.

The dispatch conversation lifecycle maps directly to Aion's activity state machine: conversation opened maps to ActivityScheduled, worker selected maps to ActivityStarted, response received maps to ActivityCompleted, and link failure maps to ActivityFailed with retry. The Aion recorder observes these transitions as channel operations, making them replayable by the resolver during workflow recovery.

Signal Delivery

Each running workflow has a dedicated signal channel, created when the workflow starts and torn down when it completes. External clients (or other workflows) deliver signals by publishing to the workflow's signal channel. The channel validates signal payloads against the workflow's declared signal types (ADR-002 typed channels). The workflow process subscribes to its signal channel and receives signals as standard BEAM messages, integrating with the existing selective-receive signal handling.

Signal channels are ephemeral by default -- signals are delivered to the running workflow process, not stored. For workflows that need signal replay during recovery, the signal channel can be configured as durable, with the recorder capturing signal deliveries as replayable events.

Event Streaming

Workflow history is published to a per-workflow history channel as events occur. The recorder publishes each event (ActivityScheduled, ActivityCompleted, SignalReceived, TimerFired, etc.) to the history channel after writing it to the event store. History consumers subscribe to the channel for live streaming, or read from the durable channel's stream for replay from an arbitrary position.

This decouples history consumers from the storage layer. Operators, monitoring dashboards, and debugging tools subscribe to history channels instead of querying the event store directly. The event store remains the source of truth for recovery; the history channel is the read path for observation.

Worker Registration

Workers register by subscribing to task queue channels. A worker declares its capacity (max concurrent activities, activity type filter) in its subscription metadata. The channel's routing function uses this metadata to make dispatch decisions. Worker health is implicit in subscription liveness -- a worker that crashes drops its subscription, and the channel immediately stops routing to it. No heartbeat protocol, no health check endpoint, no registration service.

Worker scaling is additive: start a new worker process, it subscribes to the task queue channel, the routing function begins including it in dispatch decisions. No rebalancing. Scaling down is equally simple: the worker closes its subscription (or crashes), and routing adapts immediately.

Channel Naming

Aion-specific channels follow a deterministic naming convention: aion.dispatch.{namespace}.{task_queue} for activity dispatch, aion.signal.{namespace}.{workflow_id} for signal delivery, aion.history.{namespace}.{workflow_id} for event streaming. The naming module provides constructors that enforce this convention and prevent ad-hoc channel name construction.

Recorder Integration

All channel operations in the integration surface are recorded operations. The Aion recorder captures dispatch conversations, signal deliveries, and history publications as events in the workflow's event log. The resolver can replay these operations deterministically during workflow recovery, using the recorded channel responses instead of re-executing the actual dispatch. This is the same record-replay pattern Aion uses for all non-deterministic operations -- the integration surface does not introduce a new replay mechanism.

principles.text: The integration surface is a consumer, not a fork. It uses liminal's public channel and conversation APIs and Aion's public workflow APIs. It never reaches into either system's internals.

Failure detection is structural. Worker crashes are detected via process links, not heartbeats or timeouts. If beamr can detect it, the integration surface uses that detection, never a polling substitute.

Channel operations are recorded operations. Every interaction with liminal that affects workflow determinism (dispatch, signal receipt, history read during replay) is captured by the recorder and replayable by the resolver.
Worker scaling is subscription, not configuration. Adding or removing workers requires no coordination, no rebalancing, and no configuration changes -- only channel subscription and unsubscription.

Zero-hop when co-located. Embedded Aion and liminal in the same beamr instance dispatch activities through in-process channels with no serialization or network boundary. The same code path works over beamr distribution for remote workers.
No silent fallbacks. A failed dispatch is a failed dispatch, not a silently retried one. Retry policy is Aion's decision (via its retry configuration), not the integration surface's.

goals: Activity dispatch from a workflow to a worker completes through a liminal conversation with no gRPC dependency.
Worker crash during activity execution is detected via process link and triggers re-dispatch within the same conversation lifecycle, with zero timeout delay.
Signal delivery to a running workflow goes through a typed liminal channel with payload validation at publish time.
Workflow history events are available as a subscribable channel stream, decoupling history consumers from direct event store access.
Adding a worker to the pool requires only starting the worker process and subscribing to the task queue channel -- no configuration change, no rebalancing.
All channel operations in the integration surface are replayable by Aion's resolver during workflow recovery.

non_goals.text: Modifying Aion's core workflow engine or recorder
non_goals.reason: The integration surface is a consumer of Aion's public API. Internal changes to Aion are out of scope.

non_goals.text: Implementing the liminal channel or conversation primitives
non_goals.reason: Covered by the core cluster (RM-001). This cluster assumes those primitives exist.
Aion's namespace isolation (B-053/B-054) is preserved. Dispatch channels are namespace-scoped. Cross-namespace routing is a future policy decision.

non_goals.text: Wire protocol encoding for remote Aion workers
non_goals.reason: Covered by the protocol cluster (RM-004). Remote workers use beamr distribution, which handles serialization.
Aion's existing timeout mechanism (schedule_to_close, start_to_close) is unchanged. The integration surface reports timeouts as conversation errors; it does not implement a separate timer.
Queries are synchronous reads of workflow state, not routed through channels. They remain direct calls to the workflow process.

structure.note: Module root, pub mod and pub use only
Shared types: ActivityRequest, ActivityResult, SignalPayload, WorkerCapacity, HistoryEvent envelope
Channel naming conventions and lifecycle helpers for Aion-specific channels
AionSurfaceError and conversion impls for dispatch, signal, and streaming failures
Activity dispatch conversation: worker selection, linking, completion tracking, re-dispatch on crash
Message encoding and type definitions for Aion channel message types
Signal channel creation, delivery, typed validation, and optional durable mode
History event streaming: publisher, subscriber, cursor-based replay from durable channel
Worker registration via channel subscription, capacity declaration, health-by-liveness

constraints.text: No file exceeds 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains only pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic, no type definitions, no function bodies.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error via thiserror.
Activity dispatch must complete through a liminal conversation, never by direct function call to a worker. The conversation mediates lifecycle, failure, and recording.
All non-deterministic channel operations must be recorder-compatible: the recorder can capture them and the resolver can replay them without re-executing the actual dispatch.
Channel names follow the aion.{kind}.{namespace}.{id} convention enforced by the channels module. No ad-hoc string construction of channel names outside that module.
Worker failure detection uses beamr process links exclusively. No heartbeat timers, no health-check polling, no gRPC keepalive.
